page: Vultr voc-s-4c-32gb-640s-amd is an AMD-powered virtual server belonging to the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features 4 shared vCPUs mapped to 4 physical cores of an AMD EPYC-Rome processor running at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Local storage is provided via a bundled 640 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks indicate a strong single-core capability, with stress-ng single-core CPU performance ranking in the top tier. However, multi-core scaling is more moderate, with stress-ng multi-core and PassMark CPU scores falling into the average tier. Memory bandwidth is stable, averaging 86.9 GB/sec for large block reads and 60.95 GB/sec for large block writes. The shared CPU allocation introduces some performance tradeoffs, as seen in the weaker multi-threaded bzip3 decompression and database operations benchmarks, which both score in the bottom 25%.

Given its high memory density and local NVMe storage, this server offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, dedicated multi-core CPU execution. It is well-suited for caching layers, Redis deployments, and medium-sized databases. It is less optimal for heavy multi-threaded processing, static web serving, or large-scale LLM inference due to the shared CPU architecture and lower multi-core benchmark results.
description: Vultr voc-s-4c-32gb-640s-amd is an AMD EPYC-Rome virtual server within the Optimized Cloud Compute family, configured with 4 shared vCPUs, 32.0 GB of RAM, and 640 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. Operating at 2.0 GHz, the processor delivers top-tier single-core performance in stress-ng benchmarks, though multi-core performance and memory bandwidth remain in the average tier. The high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core, combined with fast local NVMe storage, makes this instance a cost-efficient choice for memory-bound applications. However, the shared CPU allocation presents tradeoffs, resulting in weaker performance for multi-threaded decompression and database operations. This server is best suited for memory-intensive workloads such as Redis caching, medium-sized databases, and general-purpose applications requiring a larger memory footprint without intensive, sustained multi-core compute demands.
